strict_win_deltas.delta: P1 builds a single `validatedItems` array during validation and reuses it unchanged for both counting and storage (`importedItems = validatedItems.map(...)`), while P2 validates against `item.name.trim()` but then re-derives the persisted objects from the raw `batch` array with `name: item.name` (untrimmed), creating a mismatch between what was checked and what is stored.

P2 guards its `nextId` computation with `typeof item.id === 'number' && Number.isFinite(item.id)` before calling `Math.max`, while P1's `items.reduce((max, item) => Math.max(max, item.id), 0)` has no such guard and would propagate `NaN` into every generated id if any existing item lacked a valid numeric `id`.

The spec defines validity via the trimmed name and P1 persists that same trimmed value (`name: item.name.trim()`), with a test asserting `'  gamma  '` is stored as `'gamma'`, whereas P2 validates on the trimmed name but stores `item.name` untrimmed, so `GET /items` would return whitespace-padded names that no test in P2 covers.

P1 threads one sanitized `validatedItems` array through both the count and the stored objects, so a future change to the sanitize step propagates automatically, while P2 walks `batch` twice — once to check `item.name.trim()` and again to build `{ name: item.name, qty: item.qty }` from the raw batch — letting the two passes drift, which is exactly why P2's stored name isn't trimmed.
